Jacob's life is ending, but his grip on God's promises is unshakening. The old patriarch, blind and feeble, summons his last strength not for himself but to bless the future. Notice what he does first: he rehearses what God told him at Luz, the promise that his descendants would inherit Canaan forever. Jacob is not blessing his grandsons based on sentiment or favoritism. He is positioning them within a covenant narrative that stretches back to Abraham and extends into a future he will not see. When Joseph brings his sons near, Jacob adopts them as his own—not merely as an act of kindness, but as a way of binding them into the inheritance structure of God's people. This is how God's purposes advance: through an old man's faithfulness to what God has promised, sustained across decades of separation and suffering.

But then Jacob does something that stops Joseph cold. He crosses his arms and places his right hand—the hand of blessing and strength—on Ephraim, the younger son, passing over Manasseh the firstborn. Joseph objects with immediate intensity. He knows the law of primogeniture. He knows what the firstborn should receive. Yet Jacob refuses to move his hand. "I know, my son, I know!" he insists. These words cut to the heart of what Jacob has learned through his own life. Jacob himself was the younger, chosen over Esau. He has spent a lifetime wrestling with God and with himself, and he knows that God's ways overturn human expectation and birth order. Jacob is not making a sentimental choice; he is confessing that God's election operates according to His own purposes, not according to what seems right to us. The younger shall be greater—this becomes the pattern God will work throughout Israel's history.

What happens next is remarkable: Joseph's resistance melts. He accepts his father's strange blessing and lets it stand. He does not fight to change it. In that moment, Joseph demonstrates something deeper than the natural desire to honor the firstborn—he accepts that God's purposes cannot be grasped or controlled, only received. Jacob's blessing becomes Israel's blessing, the standard by which parents will pray for their children: "May God make you like Ephraim and Manasseh." And it all occurs in the shadow of death. Jacob speaks of his approaching end not with despair but with confidence that God will bring Joseph's family back to the land He promised. The covenant does not rest on Jacob's continued life or strength. It rests on God's faithfulness, which moves through generations, overturns earthly expectations, and proves itself trustworthy even when we cannot see the way ahead.
